content.text: An inner-source program without a clear ownership model collapses under one of two failure modes: either a single overloaded platform engineer reviews everything and becomes a bottleneck, or review authority is diffuse and inconsistent changes slip through. The maintainer stewardship model defines who owns what, how ownership is acquired and transferred, what obligations ownership carries, and what happens when an owner goes inactive. This document covers that model for the GenAI platform tool suite.
Roadmap priorities, resource allocation, deprecation decisions
Architecture decisions, security escalations, TC elections, release gating
Component Owner(s) per repository or sub-path
Code review approval for their module, issue triage, documentation
Any engineer with a merged contribution
No formal authority; may review but cannot approve
Ownership is recorded in two places:
for GitHub's automated review routing, and
for the human-readable record that includes contact information, time-zone, and backup owners. Both must be kept in sync —
requires contributors to manually cc reviewers on every PR.

# MAINTAINERS.md

## Module ownership

| Module path            | Primary owner      | Backup owner      | Time-zone | Since      |
|------------------------|--------------------|-------------------|-----------|------------|
| src/core/              | TC Council         | —                 | —         | 2024-01-01 |
| src/adapters/openai.py | jane.doe@co.com    | TC Council        | UTC-5     | 2024-03-15 |
| src/adapters/anthropic | john.smith@co.com  | TC Council        | UTC+0     | 2024-05-01 |
| src/adapters/bedrock.py| alice.jones@co.com | john.smith@co.com | UTC-8     | 2025-11-20 |
| src/scorers/           | TC Council         | —                 | —         | 2024-01-01 |
| prompt-registry/       | bob.chen@co.com    | jane.doe@co.com   | UTC+8     | 2024-07-01 |
| eval-runner/           | sara.kim@co.com    | TC Council        | UTC-5     | 2025-02-10 |

## Trusted Committers

| Name            | GitHub handle | Time-zone | TC since   | Status    |
|-----------------|---------------|-----------|------------|-----------|
| Jane Doe        | @janedoe      | UTC-5     | 2024-01-01 | Active    |
| John Smith      | @jsmith       | UTC+0     | 2024-01-01 | Active    |
| Alice Jones     | @alicejones   | UTC-8     | 2025-06-01 | Active    |
| Carlos Rivera   | @crivera      | UTC-6     | 2023-09-01 | Emeritus  |

## Emeritus TCs
Emeritus TCs retain read access, acknowledgement in release notes, and the
right to re-activate by petitioning the TC Council after 6 months.
Ownership is not honorary. A component owner who consistently misses obligations will have their module reassigned by the TC Council after a documented warning. The obligations are:
Triage new issues on your module within 3 business days — label them, close duplicates, and escalate if the issue is out of scope.
Provide initial PR review feedback within 5 business days of a contributor opening a PR targeting your module.
Ensure your module passes CI after every merge — a broken CI on main blocks all other contributors.
Keep your module's documentation in sync with code changes — stale docs cost contributors more time than missing docs.
Attend the monthly TC sync meeting or send a written update if absent.
Notify the TC Council if you will be unavailable for more than 10 consecutive business days, so backup coverage can be arranged.
A contributor earns component ownership by demonstrating sustained, high-quality engagement with a specific module. The TC Council votes on ownership grants — they are not automatic.
At least 3 accepted PRs to the module within 12 months
Has triaged or responded to at least 5 issues on the module
Has provided substantive review comments on at least 3 PRs from others (not just approvals)
Has improved or written at least one piece of documentation for the module
At least 1 active TC nominates the contributor and vouches for their module understanding
A TC opens a GitHub Discussion titled 'Ownership nomination: <name> for <module>'.
The TC Council has 5 business days to vote (+1 / -1 / abstain) in the discussion thread.
A simple majority of active TCs (excluding abstentions) is required to grant ownership.
On approval: add the new owner to CODEOWNERS and MAINTAINERS.md in a single PR, merged by the nominating TC.
Announce in the platform Slack channel; add to the next release note.
Owners leave teams, change roles, or reduce their platform engagement. Succession planning prevents a module from falling into the TC Council's catch-all bucket with no dedicated owner.

## Ownership transfer checklist

1. Owner identifies a successor (ideally the backup owner listed in MAINTAINERS.md).
2. Owner works with successor through at least 2 PR reviews and 1 issue triage cycle.
3. Owner opens a PR updating CODEOWNERS and MAINTAINERS.md:
   - Move current owner to "Backup" column
   - Set successor as "Primary"
   - Add handover date
4. TC Council approves the PR (no vote required for planned transfers; 1 TC approval is enough).
5. Owner announces transfer in Slack with a handover summary:
   - Open issues to watch
   - Pending feature work in flight
   - Known technical debt
   - Any external dependencies or upgrade timelines
If an owner becomes unreachable and the module's CI is failing or a critical PR is blocked, the TC Council activates the gap protocol:
TC Council declares the module 'ownership-gap' and adds the label to all open issues.
The backup owner (if listed) is contacted first and given 3 business days to confirm availability.
If no backup is available, the TC Council takes temporary ownership and opens a nomination thread for a new primary owner.
A 'help-wanted: ownership' label is posted on the module's tracking issue; the platform Slack channel is notified.
Temporary TC ownership is capped at 90 days — after that, if no new owner is found, the module is placed in 'TC-only' maintenance mode (bug fixes only, no new features).
The TC Council reviews module stewardship health monthly. A module in poor health is flagged for intervention before it reaches a crisis.

# scripts/stewardship-health.py
# Generates a monthly stewardship health report from the GitHub API

import httpx, datetime, os

REPO   = "internal/genai-llm-sdk"
HEADERS= {"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['GITHUB_TOKEN']}",
          "Accept": "application/vnd.github.v3+json"}

def get_open_issues():
    resp = httpx.get(f"https://github.internal/api/v3/repos/{REPO}/issues",
                     params={"state": "open", "per_page": 100}, headers=HEADERS)
    return resp.json()

def median_first_response_days(issues: list) -> float:
    deltas = []
    for issue in issues:
        created = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(issue["created_at"].replace("Z",""))
        # Use first comment as proxy for first response
        if issue.get("comments", 0) > 0:
            comments_url = issue["comments_url"]
            first_comment = httpx.get(comments_url, params={"per_page": 1},
                                      headers=HEADERS).json()
            if first_comment:
                commented = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(
                    first_comment[0]["created_at"].replace("Z",""))
                deltas.append((commented - created).days)
    return sum(deltas) / len(deltas) if deltas else 0

issues = get_open_issues()
unlabelled = [i for i in issues if not i["labels"]]
print(f"Open issues:               {len(issues)}")
print(f"Unlabelled:                {len(unlabelled)} ({100*len(unlabelled)/max(len(issues),1):.0f}%)")
print(f"Median first response:     {median_first_response_days(issues):.1f} days")

The GOVERNANCE.md file is the canonical reference for how decisions are made. It is linked from CONTRIBUTING.md and README.md. It does not need to be long — it needs to be unambiguous on four questions: who decides, how decisions are made, how the TC roster changes, and how disputes are resolved.

# Governance

## Decision types

| Type                  | Who decides         | Process                              |
|-----------------------|---------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Bug fix               | Component Owner     | Merge after 1 TC approval            |
| Feature addition      | Component Owner     | Merge after 2 TC approvals           |
| Breaking change       | TC Council          | RFC + TC vote (simple majority)      |
| Deprecation           | TC Council + PO     | RFC + 7-day comment period           |
| Architecture change   | TC Council          | ADR required; TC vote (2/3 majority) |
| TC election/removal   | TC Council          | Secret vote; 2/3 majority            |

## RFC process (for breaking changes and architecture decisions)
1. Author opens a GitHub Discussion with the label `rfc`.
2. 7-day comment period — any engineer may comment.
3. TC Council votes at the end of the comment period.
4. Accepted RFCs become Architecture Decision Records in `docs/adr/`.

## Dispute resolution
1. Module owner and contributor attempt to resolve directly.
2. If unresolved after 5 days, either party escalates to the TC Council.
3. TC Council mediates and issues a binding decision within 5 business days.
4. Either party may escalate to the Platform Product Owner if they believe the
   TC Council decision is outside the program's charter.

## TC removal
A TC may be removed by a 2/3 vote of the remaining TCs if they:
- Repeatedly merge code that violates security policy.
- Are abusive toward contributors in any forum.
- Have been inactive for > 6 months without an Emeritus declaration.

The TC Council is not a committee that meets when problems arise — it has a predictable operating rhythm that contributors can count on. Consistency matters: if contributors know that their RFC will be reviewed at a fixed cadence, they plan around it rather than pinging TCs individually.
All open PRs with no TC activity in 5+ days
Review assignments; reminders to blocked contributors
Health metrics report, open issues by module, contributor activity
Flagged modules, ownership gaps opened, intervention plans
Accept/reject decisions recorded in GitHub; ADRs drafted
Emeritus transitions, new TC nominations opened
Contribution stats, health trends, contributor survey
Program improvements, updated CONTRIBUTING.md, next year's roadmap
Monthly TC Sync Meeting Agenda Template

# TC Sync — [Month Year]

**Duration:** 60 minutes
**Notes doc:** link-to-shared-doc

## Agenda

1. (5 min)  CI status — any modules with broken main?
2. (10 min) Stewardship health metrics review
   - Modules in Warning or Critical state
   - Owner response time outliers
3. (15 min) RFC votes
   - [RFC #] Title — present rationale, call for questions, vote
4. (10 min) Ownership changes
   - Nominations pending
   - Gap protocol activations
5. (10 min) Open contributor questions (surfaced from Slack/GitHub)
6. (5 min)  Release schedule — next planned tag
7. (5 min)  AOB

## Action items from last meeting
- [ ] ...

Inner-source programs that rely purely on altruism stall. Product engineers have sprint commitments and manager expectations that pull against unplanned platform contributions. Recognition structures make the value exchange explicit and visible to managers.
Every release note lists contributors by name with a link to their merged PRs. This is the minimum — it costs nothing and signals that contribution is visible.

The platform team's monthly scorecard reports inner-source activity per team (number of contributors, merged PRs, owned modules). Teams that contribute appear in the score; teams that only consume do not.
The top three contributors by merged PRs receive public recognition in the engineering all-hands and a budget allocation for a technical conference of their choice.

Becoming a TC is mapped explicitly to the senior engineer / staff engineer progression. Managers are briefed that TC activity counts toward the 'technical leadership' dimension of performance reviews.

The platform team provides managers with a one-page summary of what inner-source contribution involves and how to account for it in sprint planning — removing the friction of managers blocking contribution time.
Owner is listed in CODEOWNERS but never reviews; PRs wait indefinitely
Monthly health check flags owners with > 10-day response time; TC opens gap protocol at 60 days
TCs approve every PR without substantive feedback; security issue slips through
Require a written rationale in at least 1 review comment per PR; security scan failure is a hard block
One TC is listed as owner of every sensitive module; vacation creates a merge queue of 20 PRs
Enforce a minimum of 2 TCs per module in CODEOWNERS; backup TC is a requirement, not optional
Owner adds their name to modules they wrote years ago but no longer actively maintain; health metrics report 8 modules for one person
TC Council conducts quarterly capacity review; owners may hold at most 2 primary + 2 backup slots
Primary owner leaves the company; module has no documented successor and TC Council has no starting point
MAINTAINERS.md backup column is required before a module can be declared healthy; CI warns if it is blank

The maintainer stewardship model makes ownership explicit, accountable, and transferable. Every module has a named primary owner, a backup, and a set of measurable health metrics that the TC Council reviews monthly. Ownership is earned through demonstrated contribution and granted by TC vote — not assigned by management or grabbed informally. When an owner goes inactive, the gap protocol activates automatically before the module degrades. The GOVERNANCE.md file records how decisions are made and how disputes are resolved, so contributors and owners alike have a clear reference rather than relying on institutional memory. Together, these structures let the inner-source program scale beyond its founding team without losing the quality consistency that makes the platform worth contributing to.
